RUBRICS, EXEMPLARS, AND CRITERIA

Chapter 7 — Rubrics, Exemplars, and Crit — The student reads this and learns nothing specific. "Strong analytical thinking" is not observable. "Minor areas for dev

beats.text: A student submits an essay and receives a grade of B+ with the rubric notation: "Analysis: Good. Demonstrates strong analytical thinking with minor areas for development." The student reads this and learns nothing specific. "Strong analytical thinking" is not observable. "Minor areas for development" names a gap without locating it.

segments.label: 01 / 09 · HOOK

title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.
segments.body_html:
·Identify vague language in rubrics and convert it into observable criteria with Claude's help
·Draft analytic and single-point rubrics with Claude, then audit them before use
·Generate common-failure exemplars that show students what to avoid
·Create student-facing checklists from complex rubrics
·Apply a six-question rubric audit before distributing any Claude-drafted assessment tool

beats.text: By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Identify vague language in rubrics and convert it into observable criteria with Claude's help Draft analytic and single-point rubrics with Claude, then audit them before use Generate common-failure exemplars that show students what to avoid Create student-facing checklists from complex rubrics Apply a six-question rubric audit before distributing any Claude-drafted assessment tool

segments.label: 02 / 09 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: CORE CONCEPTS
segments.body_html: A rubric→Criteria→Performance levels→Exemplars→Assessment transparency

beats.text: A rubric is a scoring guide that lists criteria and describes performance levels. The two most common forms are analytic rubrics (separate criteria scored independently, then summed) and single-point rubrics (one column describes the target quality; the teacher notes what is missing or exceeds it). Heidi Andrade's research on rubric use shows that rubrics improve student performance when students use them before and during work, not only after it (Andrade, rubric research).

segments.label: 03 / 09 · CORE CONCEPTS

title: WORKED WALKTHROUGH:
FROM VAGUE RUBRIC TO OBSERVABLE CRITERIA
segments.body_html:
Argument — Strong, clear argument
Evidence — Excellent use of evidence
Analysis — Demonstrates sophisticated analysis

beats.text: A university writing instructor uses this rubric for argumentative essays: This rubric has four criteria. All four use quality words to describe quality. A student who receives "Good" in Analysis knows they are not in the top band and not in the bottom band. They do not know what they need to do differently.

segments.label: 04 / 09 · WORKED WALKTHROUGH: FROM VAGUE

title: THE HUMAN
GATE.

Is student language clear? A rubric written for your own grading logic may not work as a student-facing tool. Test itgive it to a student (or a colleague unfamiliar with the assignment) and ask whether they could use it to self-assess be

beats.text: The six-question rubric audit, applied before distributing any Claude-drafted rubric or exemplar: Are criteria observable? A reviewer of the student work and a reader of the rubric should be able to agree on whether the criterion is met , without additional interpretation from the teacher.

segments.label: 05 / 09 · THE HUMAN GATE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Accepting Claude's quality words.→Using AI-generated exemplars without review.→Building rubrics before defining the outcome.→Letting the rubric expand beyond what you actually grade.→Failing to share the rubric before the assignment.

beats.text: Accepting Claude's quality words. If you ask Claude to write performance level descriptions and do not specify that they must be observable, Claude will often produce the same adjective-chain problem the original rubric had. The prompt must specify: no quality words, observable behaviors only. Using AI-generated exemplars without review. Claude's constructed examples may contain factual errors, misrepresent the assignment, or model a failure in a way that is unclear. Review every exemplar before distributing it.

segments.label: 06 / 09 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE
segments.body_html: Rubrics clarify what quality looks like when students are working. Chapter 8 takes up the harder question that AI forces

beats.text: Rubrics clarify what quality looks like when students are working. Chapter 8 takes up the harder question that AI forces on every teacher: when a student can use Claude to produce the artifact the rubric describes, does the rubric still measure learning? Assessment in the age of Claude requires looking beyond the artifact to the thinking that should have produced it.
